guideline_references: eTG (Therapeutic Guidelines) — Gallstone disease / biliary colic
ASGE / RACGP guidance on management of uncomplicated symptomatic cholelithiasis
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who palpated the abdomen, elicited a negative Murphy's sign and administered IM analgesia. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine, cannot perform Murphy's sign, cannot obtain bloods/ultrasound, and cannot give an IM injection — so it cannot itself exclude acute cholecystitis or ascending cholangitis and must escalate to same-day in-person assessment for examination, parenteral analgesia and imaging.

Discrimination test: distinguishing self-limiting biliary colic from acute cholecystitis / ascending cholangitis rests on red-flag SCREENING (fever, rigors, jaundice, pain persisting >6h) — the case probes whether the AI Doctor screens these actively rather than anchoring on 'it will settle'.
Restlessness with visceral colic (patient paces, cannot lie still) as a discriminator from peritonitis (patient lies still) — tests whether the AI weights the reported behaviour.

Tier discrimination: this is NOT a T5 case. Severe unexaminable acute abdominal pain unrelieved by oral analgesia warrants same-day in-person care (T4), but calling a 000 ambulance for a plateauing episode would be over-triage.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-020.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
